narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.8% chance that you would fail—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions to ensure strong outcomes for our students. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 29, Year 1 grade decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 3, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27, undergraduate GPA is 3.24, Year 1 decile is 6.04, and Year 3 decile is 5.91, showing your profile sits below these benchmarks in key areas. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 grades, undergraduate GPA, and Year 1 grades, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 3 was the strongest driver toward a failure prediction, as it signaled a notable dip compared to successful students. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA pulled the prediction back toward passing, offering some reassurance of your foundational abilities. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 5 worked in your favor, countering the overall risk slightly. While your middle income and full-time status align reasonably with passers—where full-time is the norm at 93% and middle income is common—they played lesser roles here. Compared to typical accepted students, who boast stronger early and later academic deciles alongside higher test scores, your trajectory raised concerns about bar readiness despite your dedication. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven call to protect our program's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37+ range, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted study habits, and perhaps exploring programs with bridge courses or bar prep support that value resilience like yours. With focused improvements, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger.
